|| Holarctic, except for Greenland. || The size of the nominate subspecies of mallard varies [[Cline (biology)|clinally]].{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RT8qww1cyUMC&q=A.+p.+conboschas&pg=PA102|title=Wildfowl of the World|last1=Ogilvie|first1=M. A.|last2=Young|first2=Steve|date=2002|publisher=New Holland Publishers|isbn=978-1-84330-328-2|language=en}}{{Dead link|date=August 2024 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} Note that [[domesticated duck]]s, although often cited as "A. p. domesticus", do ''not'' comprise a valid distinct subspecies; they are derived from within ''A. p. platyrhynchos''. |- |} In the past, several other ducks, now treated as distinct species, were sometimes treated as subspecies of mallard:{{cite book | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World: Volume 1 Ostrich to Ducks | publisher=Lynx edicions | publication-place=Barcelona | date=1992 | isbn=84-87334-10-5 | pages=605–606}} *[[Laysan duck]] ''Anas laysanensis'' *[[Hawaiian duck]] ''Anas wyvilliana'' *[[Mottled duck]] ''Anas fulvigula'' *[[American black duck]] ''Anas rubripes'' *[[Mexican duck]] ''Anas diazi'' ==Description== The mallard is a medium-sized waterfowl species that is often slightly heavier than most other dabbling ducks. It is {{convert|50|–|65|cm|in|abbr=on}} long{{snd}}of which the body makes up around two-thirds{{snd}}has a wingspan of {{convert|81|–|98|cm|in|abbr=on}},{{r|cramp|page=505}} and weighs {{convert|0.7|–|1.6|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. Among standard measurements, the [[Wing chord (biology)|wing chord]] is {{convert|25.7|to|30.6|cm|in|abbr=on}}, the [[Beak|bill]] is {{convert|4.4|to|6.1|cm|in|abbr=on}}, and the [[Tarsus (skeleton)|tarsus]] is {{convert|4.1|to|4.8|cm|in|abbr=on}}.[[File:DrakeMallardPortrait.png|thumb|left|Adult drake mallard|220x220px]]The breeding male mallard is unmistakable, with a glossy bottle-green head and a white collar that demarcates the head and neck from the purple-tinged brown breast, grey-brown wings, and a pale grey belly.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RT8qww1cyUMC&q=mallard+bottle-green+white+collar&pg=PA102|title=Wildfowl of the World|last1=Ogilvie|first1=M. A.|last2=Young|first2=Steve|date=2002|publisher=New Holland Publishers|isbn=978-1-84330-328-2|language=en}}{{Dead link|date=August 2024 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} The rear of the male is black, with white-bordered dark tail feathers.{{r|cramp|page=506}} The bill of the male is a yellowish-orange tipped with black, with that of the female generally darker and ranging from black to mottled orange and brown.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wG18DQAAQBAJ&q=mallard+bottle-green+white+collar&pg=PA22|title=Birds of Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East: A Photographic Guide|last1=Jiguet|first1=Frédéric|last2=Audevard|first2=Aurélien|date=21 March 2017|publisher=Princeton University Press|isbn=978-0-691-17243-9|language=en}} The female mallard is predominantly mottled, with each individual feather showing sharp contrast from buff to very dark brown, a coloration shared by most female dabbling ducks, and has buff cheeks, eyebrow, throat, and neck, with a darker crown and eye-stripe.{{r|cramp|page=506}} Mallards, like other [[Sexual dimorphism|sexually-dimorphic]] birds, can sometimes go through spontaneous sex reversal,{{Cite journal |last1=Rayner |first1=Enid |last2=Hamra |first2=Wayne |last3=Shipton |first3=Warren |date=2015 |title=Sexual Dimorphism and Sex Reversal in Birds |url=https://www.academia.edu/70676100 |journal=Journal of Applied Animal Science |volume=8 |issue=3 |pages=27–34}} often caused by damaged or nonfunctioning sex organs, such as the [[Ovary|ovaries]].{{Cite journal |last1=Lavretsky |first1=Philip |last2=Hernandez |first2=Flor |last3=Davis |first3=Brian |date=2022 |title=Ovarian degeneration resulting in the phenotypic masculinisation of a wild female Mallard Anas platyrhynchos |url=https://wildfowl.wwt.org.uk/index.php/wildfowl/article/view/2777 |journal=Wildfowl |volume=72}} This phenomenon can cause female mallards to exhibit male plumage, and vice versa (phenotypic feminisation or masculinisation). Both male and female mallards have distinct iridescent purple-blue speculum feathers edged with white, which are prominent in flight or at rest but temporarily shed during the annual summer moult.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=T4gLRnplhVwC&q=mallard+eating+frog+google+book&pg=PA150|title=Wildlife of Pennsylvania and the Northeast|last1=Fergus|first1=Charles|last2=Hansen|first2=Amelia|date=2000|publisher=Stackpole Books|isbn=978-0-8117-2899-7|language=en}} {{Multiple image | image1 = Drake mallard flight (cropped).jpg | image2 = Nova Scotia DSC 1316 (2278772065) (cropped 2).jpg | total_width = 250 | direction = vertical | footer = Showing [[speculum feathers]]:
male (top) and female (bottom) }} Upon hatching, the plumage of the duckling is yellow on the underside and face (with streaks by the eyes) and black on the back (with some yellow spots) all the way to the top and back of the head.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=FYbrCAAAQBAJ&q=mallard+the+plumage++duckling+is+yellow&pg=PA323|title=The inheritance of plumage colour in the common duck (Anas platyrhynchos linné)|last=Lancaster|first=Frank Maurice|date=17 December 2013|publisher=Springer|isbn=978-94-017-6834-4|language=en}} Its legs and bill are also black. As it nears a month in age, the duckling's plumage starts becoming drab, looking more like the female, though more streaked, and its legs lose their dark grey colouring.{{r|cramp|page=506}} Two months after hatching, the fledgling period has ended, and the duckling is now a juvenile.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=e95PAAAAYAAJ|title=Annual Report|last=Station|first=Delta Waterfowl and Wetlands Research|date=1984|publisher=The Station|language=en}} The duckling is able to fly 50–60 days after hatching. Its bill soon loses its dark grey colouring, and its sex can finally be distinguished visually by three factors: 1){{nbsp}}the bill is yellow in males, but black and orange in females;{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kU9XBQAAQBAJ&q=mallard+The+centre+tail+feather+is+curled+for+males+(called+a+drake+feather),+straight+for+females&pg=PT16|title=Daisy and Ducky Mallard|last=Moulton|first=Judy|date=7 November 2014|publisher=Xlibris Corporation|isbn=978-1-5035-1191-0|language=en}}{{self-published source|date=December 2017}} 2){{nbsp}}the breast feathers are reddish-brown in males, but brown in females; and 3){{nbsp}}in males, the centre tail feather (drake feather) is curled, but in females, the centre tail feather is straight. During the final period of maturity leading up to adulthood (6–10 months of age), the plumage of female juveniles remains the same while the plumage of male juveniles gradually changes to its characteristic colours.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=9eHzAgAAQBAJ&q=mallard+plumage+of+female+juveniles&pg=PA51|title=The Helm Guide to Bird Identification|last=Vinicombe|first=Keith|date=27 March 2014|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4729-0554-3|language=en}} This change in plumage also applies to adult mallard males when they transition in and out of their non-breeding [[eclipse plumage]] at the beginning and the end of the summer moulting period. The adulthood age for mallards is fourteen months, and the average life expectancy is three years, but they can live to twenty.[[File:Anas Rubripes and Anas Platyrhynchos August 2008.JPG|thumb|An [[American black duck]] (upper left) and a male mallard (lower right) in eclipse plumage]]Several species of duck have brown-plumaged females that can be confused with the female mallard.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ToFfBL6e9zwC&q=mallard+Several+species+of+duck+have+brown-plumaged+females&pg=PA78|title=Attracting Birds to Your Garden|last1=Moss|first1=Stephen|last2=Cottridge|first2=David|date=2000|publisher=New Holland Publishers|isbn=978-1-85974-005-7|language=en}}{{Dead link|date=August 2024 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} The female [[gadwall]] (''Mareca strepera'') has an orange-lined bill, white belly, black and white speculum that is seen as a white square on the wings in flight, and is a smaller bird.{{r|cramp|page=506}} More similar to the female mallard in [[North America]] are the American black duck (''A.{{nbsp}}rubripes''), which is notably darker-hued in both sexes than the mallard,{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=VEbX_iVqWrUC&q=mallard+More+similar+to+the+female+mallard+in+North+America+are+the+American+black+duck&pg=PA26|title=Kaufman Field Guide to Birds of North America|last=Kaufman|first=Kenn|date=2005|publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt|isbn=978-0-618-57423-0|language=en}} and the [[mottled duck]] (''A.{{nbsp}}fulvigula''), which is somewhat darker than the female mallard, and with slightly different bare-part colouration and no white edge on the speculum. [[File:Female Mallard with faded plumage at Bray Lock, May 2015.JPG|thumb|Mallards are among the most common bird species to exhibit aberrant colouration, typically due to the influence of escaped domesticated ducks, more rarely to genetic mutations.{{Cite journal |last1=Abraham |first1=Kevin |last2=Risley |first2=Chris |last3=Weseloh |first3=D.V. Chip |date=2020 |title=Aberrant colouration in some Ontario birds |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/36-48%20OntBirds%20Apr%202020%2038-1b.pdf |journal=Ontario Birds |pages=36–48}} The female pictured here is [[Leucism|leucistic]]; leucism in birds often results in 'cream-coloured', 'apricot'{{Cite web |title=Apricot Mallard |url=https://chavezpark.org/apricot-mallard/ |website=Chavez Park Conservancy}} or muted feathers on certain parts of the body.{{Cite journal |last=van Grouw |first=Hein |date=2006 |title=Not every white bird is an albino: sense and nonsense about colour aberrations in birds |journal=Dutch Birding |volume=28 |pages=79–89}}]] In captivity, domestic ducks come in wild-type plumages, white, and other colours.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=njxEAAAAYAAJ&q=mallard+In+captivity,+domestic+ducks+come+in+wild-type+plumages,+white,+and+other+colours|title=The Encyclopaedia of Poultry|last=Hicks|first=James Stephen|date=1923|publisher=Waverley Book Company|language=en}} Most of these colour variants are also known in domestic mallards not bred as livestock, but kept as pets, [[aviary]] birds, etc., where they are rare but increasing in availability. The mallard is a rare example of both [[Allen's Rule]] and [[Bergmann's Rule]] in birds.{{Cite journal|last1=Salewski|first1=Volker|last2=Hochachka|first2=Wesley M.|last3=Fiedler|first3=Wolfgang|date=September 2009|title=Global warming and Bergmann's rule: do central European passerines adjust their body size to rising temperatures?|journal=Oecologia|volume=162|issue=1|pages=247–260|doi=10.1007/s00442-009-1446-2|issn=0029-8549|pmc=2776161|pmid=19722109}} Bergmann's Rule, which states that polar forms tend to be larger than related ones from warmer climates, has numerous examples in birds,{{Cite journal|author1-link=Matan Shelomi|last1=Shelomi|first1=Matan|last2=Zeuss|first2=Dirk|date=2017|title=Bergmann's and Allen's Rules in Native European and Mediterranean Phasmatodea|journal=Frontiers in Ecology and Evolution|language=en|volume=5|page=25 |doi=10.3389/fevo.2017.00025|bibcode=2017FrEEv...5...25S |issn=2296-701X|doi-access=free|hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-002C-DD87-4|hdl-access=free}} as in case of the Greenland mallard which is larger than the mallards further south. Allen's Rule says that appendages like ears tend to be smaller in polar forms to minimise heat loss, and larger in tropical and desert equivalents to facilitate heat diffusion, and that the polar taxa are stockier overall.{{Cite journal|last1=Bidau|first1=Claudio J.|last2=Martí|first2=Dardo A.|date=August 2008|title=A test of Allen's rule in ectotherms: the case of two south American Melanopline Grasshoppers (Orthoptera: Acrididae) with partially overlapping geographic ranges|journal=Neotropical Entomology|volume=37|issue=4|pages=370–380|doi=10.1590/S1519-566X2008000400004|pmid=18813738|issn=1519-566X|doi-access=free|hdl=11336/61319|hdl-access=free}} Examples of this rule in birds are rare as they lack external ears, but the bill of ducks is supplied with a few blood vessels to prevent heat loss,{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kOdPAAAAYAAJ|title=Ducks Unlimited Magazine|publisher=Ducks Unlimited, Incorporated|year=2003|volume=67–68|page=62|language=en}} and, as in the Greenland mallard, the bill is smaller than that of birds farther south, illustrating the rule. Due to the variability of the mallard's genetic code, which gives it its vast interbreeding capability, mutations in the genes that decide plumage colour are very common and have resulted in a wide variety of hybrids, such as [[Brewer's duck]] (mallard × gadwall, ''Mareca strepera''). === Vocalization === {{Listen|filename=Mallard (Anas platyrhynchos) (W1CDR0001518 BD17).ogg|title=Call|description=A group of mallards quacking}}{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Anas-platyrhynchos|species=Mallard}} A noisy species, the female has the deep ''quack'' stereotypically associated with ducks.{{r|cramp|page=507}} The female will often call with a sequence of 2–10 ''quacks'' in a row, starting loud and with the volume gradually decreasing.{{Cite web |title=Mallard Sounds, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology |url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Mallard/sounds |access-date=2024-04-20 |website=www.allaboutbirds.org |language=en}} Male mallards make a sound phonetically similar to that of the female, a typical ''quack'', but it is deeper and quieter compared to that of the female. Research conducted by Middlesex University on two English mallard populations found that the vocalisations of the mallard varies depending on their environment and have something akin to a [[Accent (sociolinguistics)|regional accent]], with urban mallards in [[London]] being much louder and more vociferous compared to rural mallards in [[Cornwall]], serving as an adaptation to persistent levels of anthropogenic noise.{{Cite news |date=2004-06-04 |title=Ducks 'quack in regional accents' |language=en-GB |url=http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/uk_news/3775799.stm |access-date=2022-11-27}}{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=2004-06-04 |title=Ducks quack in cockney, study finds |url=https://www.abc.net.au/science/articles/2004/06/04/1124782.htm |access-date=2022-11-27 |website=www.abc.net.au |language=en-AU}} When [[Egg incubation|incubating]] a nest, or when offspring are present, females vocalise differently, making a call that sounds like a truncated version of the usual quack. This maternal vocalisation is highly attractive to their young. The repetition and [[Frequency|frequency modulation]] of these ''quacks'' form the auditory basis for [[Intra-species recognition|species identification]] in offspring, a process known as acoustic [[conspecific]] identification.{{Cite journal|last1=Dyer|first1=Antoinette B.|last2=Gottlieb|first2=Gilbert|date=1990|title=Auditory basis of maternal attachment in ducklings (Anas platyrhynchos) under simulated naturalistic imprinting conditions.|journal=Journal of Comparative Psychology|language=en|volume=104|issue=2|pages=190–194|doi=10.1037/0735-7036.104.2.190|pmid=2364664|issn=1939-2087}} In addition, females hiss if the nest or offspring are threatened or interfered with. When taking off, the wings of a mallard produce a characteristic faint whistling noise.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=dXwPF_5IduIC|title=Life Histories of North American Wild Fowl|last=Bent|first=Arthur Cleveland|date=1962|publisher=Courier Corporation|isbn=978-0-486-25422-7|language=en}} == Distribution and habitat == The mallard is widely distributed across the [[Northern Hemisphere|Northern]] and [[Southern Hemisphere|Southern]] Hemispheres; in North America its range extends from southern and central Alaska to Mexico, the [[Hawaiian Islands]],{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gFawO8q9VfIC&q=mallard+southern+greenland&pg=PA212|title=Wildfowl|last=Madge|first=Steve|date=2010|publisher=A&C Black|isbn=978-1-4081-3895-3|page=212|language=en}} across the [[Palearctic]],{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HVI3DQAAQBAJ|title=Birds of Seychelles|last1=Skerrett|first1=Adrian|last2=Disley|first2=Tony|date=2016|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4729-4601-0|page=48|language=en}} from Iceland{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=yffUBAAAQBAJ&q=mallard+morocco&pg=PA219|title=Birds of the Strait of Gibraltar|last=Finlayson|first=Clive|date=2010|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4081-3694-2|page=219|language=en}} and southern Greenland and parts of Morocco ([[North Africa]]) in the west, [[Scandinavia]] and Britain to the north, and to Siberia,{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=JVpEDgAAQBAJ&q=mallard+siberia&pg=PA389|title=Bird Migration across the Himalayas: Wetland Functioning amidst Mountains and Glaciers|last1=Prins|first1=Herbert H. T.|last2=Namgail|first2=Tsewang|date=6 April 2017|publisher=Cambridge University Press|isbn=978-1-107-11471-5|language=en}} Japan,{{Cite journal|last1=Yamaguchi|first1=Noriyuki|last2=Hiraoka|first2=Emiko|last3=Fujita|first3=Masaki|last4=Hijikata|first4=Naoya|last5=Ueta|first5=Mutsuyuki|last6=Takagi|first6=Kentaro|last7=Konno|first7=Satoshi|last8=Okuyama|first8=Miwa|last9=Watanabe|first9=Yuki|date=September 2008|title=Spring migration routes of mallards (Anas platyrhynchos) that winter in Japan, determined from satellite telemetry|journal=Zoological Science|volume=25|issue=9|pages=875–881|doi=10.2108/zsj.25.875|issn=0289-0003|pmid=19267595|s2cid=23445791|doi-access=free}} and South Korea. Also in the east, it ranges to south-eastern and south-western Australia{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_BjSBAAAQBAJ|title=Naturalised Birds of the World|last=Lever|first=Christopher|date=2010|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4081-3312-5|page=89|language=en}} and New Zealand{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_BjSBAAAQBAJ&pg=PA89|title=Naturalised Birds of the World|last=Lever|first=Christopher|date=2010|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4081-3312-5|page=89|language=en}} in the Southern Hemisphere.{{r|cramp|page=505}} It is strongly [[bird migration|migratory]] in the northern parts of its breeding range, and winters farther south.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gas8AAAAIAAJ|title=International Wildfowl Inquiry Volume i Factors Affecting the General Status of Wild Geese and Wild Duck|last=Anonymous|publisher=Cambridge University Press|language=en}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=GFP1a2Qo9DoC|title=Habitat Management for Migrating and Wintering Waterfowl in North America|last1=Smith|first1=Loren M.|last2=Pederson|first2=Roger L.|last3=Kaminski|first3=Richard M.|date=1989|publisher=Texas Tech University Press|isbn=978-0-89672-204-0|language=en}} For example, in North America, it winters south to the [[southern United States]] and [[northern Mexico]],{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ILrZ4fhHnGcC|title=Final environmental statement for the issuance of annual regulations permitting the sport hunting of migratory birds|last=Service|first=U. S. Fish and Wildlife|date=1975|publisher=U.S. Dept. of the Interior, U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service|language=en}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=0mHW6i4e9QIC|title=The Laguna Madre of Texas and Tamaulipas|last1=Tunnell|first1=John Wesley|last2=Judd|first2=Frank W.|date=2002|publisher=Texas A&M University Press|isbn=978-1-58544-133-4|page=180|language=en}} but also regularly strays into [[Central America]] and the [[Caribbean]] between September and May.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=3tKAfqxkFAIC|title=Westwood Lake Chronicles|last=Winkler|first=Lawrence|date=2012|publisher=Lawrence Winkler|isbn=978-0-9916941-0-5|language=en}} A drake later named "Trevor" attracted media attention in 2018 when it turned up on the island of [[Niue]], an atypical location for mallards.{{cite news |last=Mulligan |first=Jesse |date=6 September 2018 |title=The loneliest duck in Niue |url=https://www.radionz.co.nz/national/programmes/afternoons/audio/2018661388/the-loneliest-duck-in-niue |work=[[Radio New Zealand]] |access-date=29 January 2019 }}{{cite news |last=Lyons |first=Kate |date=7 September 2018 |title=Trevor the lonely duck gets tiny island of Niue in a flap|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2018/sep/07/trevor-the-lonely-duck-gets-tiny-island-of-niue-in-a-flap |work=[[The Guardian]] |access-date=29 January 2019 }} The mallard inhabits a wide range of habitats and climates, from the Arctic tundra to subtropical regions.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xNQgAQAAIAAJ|title=People and Wildlife in Northern North America: Essays in Honor of R. Dale Guthrie|last=Guthrie|first=Russell Dale|date=2001|publisher=Archaeopress|isbn=978-1-84171-236-9|language=en}} It is found in both fresh- and salt-water wetlands, including parks, small ponds, rivers, lakes and [[Estuary|estuaries]], as well as shallow inlets and open sea within sight of the coastline.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wWQw11SLvVwC&q=mallard+arctic+tundra&pg=PA1525|title=International Wildlife Encyclopedia: Leopard – marten|last1=Burton|first1=Maurice|last2=Burton|first2=Robert|date=2002|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|isbn=978-0-7614-7277-3|page=1525|language=en}} Water depths of less than {{convert|0.9|m|ft}} are preferred, with birds avoiding areas more than a few metres deep.{{cite web|url=http://www.elmwoodparkzoo.org/animal-mallard-duck.php|title=Mallard Duck • Elmwood Park Zoo {{!}} Elmwood Park Zoo {{!}} www.elmwoodparkzoo.org|date=2015|website=www.elmwoodparkzoo.org|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170218090853/http://www.elmwoodparkzoo.org/animal-mallard-duck.php|archive-date=18 February 2017|access-date=19 June 2017}} They are attracted to bodies of water with aquatic vegetation.{{r|cramp|page=507}} ==Behaviour== [[File:Drake mallard Anas platyrhynchos performing the grunt-whistle 2.jpg|thumb|Drake mallard performing the grunt-whistle]] ===Feeding=== The mallard is omnivorous and very flexible in its choice of food.{{Cite journal|last1=van Toor|first1=Mariëlle L.|last2=Hedenström|first2=Anders|last3=Waldenström|first3=Jonas|last4=Fiedler|first4=Wolfgang|last5=Holland|first5=Richard A.|last6=Thorup|first6=Kasper|last7=Wikelski|first7=Martin|date=30 August 2013|title=Flexibility of Continental Navigation and Migration in European Mallards|journal=PLOS ONE|volume=8|issue=8|doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0072629|issn=1932-6203|pmc=3758317|pmid=24023629|article-number=e72629|bibcode=2013PLoSO...872629V|doi-access=free}} Its diet may vary based on several factors, including the stage of the breeding cycle, short-term variations in available food, [[nutrient]] availability, and [[Interspecific competition|interspecific]] and [[intraspecific competition]]. The majority of the mallard's diet seems to be made up of [[gastropod]]s,{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=fnkZBQAAQBAJ&q=mallard+diet+gastropod&pg=PA410|title=Ducks, Geese, and Swans of North America|last=Baldassarre|first=Guy A.|date=2014|publisher=Johns Hopkins University Press|isbn=978-1-4214-0751-7|page=410|language=en}} [[insect]]s (including beetles, flies, [[lepidoptera]]ns, [[Dragonfly|dragonflies]], and [[Caddisfly|caddisflies]]),{{Cite journal|last=Eldridge|first=Jan|date=1990|title=Waterfowl Management Handbook|url=https://www.nwrc.usgs.gov/wdb/pub/wmh/13_3_3.pdf|journal=Fish and Wildlife Leaflet}} [[crustacean]]s,{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PGSSr19ez0kC&q=Mallards+usually+form+pairs+(in+October+and+November+in+the+Northern+hemisphere)&pg=PA102|title=Wildlife of the Mid-Atlantic: A Complete Reference Manual|last=Rappole|first=John H.|date=2012|publisher=University of Pennsylvania Press|isbn=978-0-8122-2201-2|page=102|language=en}} other [[arthropod]]s,{{cite web | url=https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Anas_platyrhynchos/ | title=''Anas platyrhynchos'' (Mallard) | website=[[Animal Diversity Web]] }} worms, [[feces]] of other birds,{{cite journal | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/mallar3/cur/foodhabits | title=Mallard (Anas platyrhynchos), version 1.0 | journal=Birds of the World | date=8 May 2024 | last1=Drilling | first1=Nancy | last2=Titman | first2=Rodger D. | last3=McKinney | first3=Frank }} many varieties of seeds and plant matter, and roots and [[tuber]]s. During the breeding season, male birds were recorded to have eaten 37.6% animal matter and 62.4% plant matter, most notably the grass ''[[Echinochloa crus-galli]]'', and nonlaying females ate 37.0% animal matter and 63.0% plant matter, while laying females ate 71.9% animal matter and only 28.1% plant matter. Plants generally make up the larger part of a bird's diet, especially during autumn migration and in the winter. The mallard usually feeds by dabbling for plant food or grazing; there are reports of it eating [[frog]]s, other [[amphibian]]s, and [[fish]], including [[Carrion|carcass]]es.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Z6Rf3otGNdUC&q=mallard+eating+frog+google+book&pg=PA60|title=Birds of Ontario: Habitat Requirements, Limiting Factors, and Status: Volume 1–Nonpasserines: Loons through Cranes|last=Sandilands|first=Al|date=2011|publisher=University of British Columbia Press|isbn=978-0-7748-5943-1|page=60|language=en}} However, in 2017 a flock of mallards in Romania were observed hunting fledglings of small migratory birds when they land in the water, which included a [[grey wagtail]] and a [[black redstart]]. This was the first documented occasion they had been seen attacking and consuming large vertebrates.{{cite web |url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/science-environment-40445379 |title=Wild ducks caught on camera snacking on small birds |last=Briggs |first=Helen |date=30 June 2017 |website=BBC News |publisher=BBC |access-date=30 June 2017 }} In 2026, this behaviour was also observed in New Zealand's [[Lake Alexandrina (New Zealand)|Lake Alexandrina]], with mallards observed preying upon the chicks of the [[pūteketeke]] and subsequently euthanised.[https://www.doc.govt.nz/news/media-releases/2026-media-releases/doc-shock-as-ducks-kill-puteketeke-chicks/ DOC shock as ducks kill pūteketeke chicks] Department of Conservation It usually nests on a river bank, but not always near water. It is highly gregarious outside of the breeding season and forms large [[Flock (birds)|flock]]s, which are known as "sordes".{{Cite book|last=Lipton|first=James|url=https://archive.org/details/exaltationoflark0000lipt_x7z9|url-access=registration|title=An Exaltation of Larks|date=1991|publisher=Viking|isbn=978-0-670-30044-0|language=en}} File:Mallard duck eating bread.jpg|Eating bread File:Fish-Eating Mallard.jpg|Eating a small fish File:Duck eating a Fish (5979923772).jpg|Eating a larger fish File:Mallard drake eating leopard frog Sand Lake WMD (14822335555).jpg|Eating a frog ===Breeding=== Mallards usually form pairs (in October and November in the Northern Hemisphere) until the female lays eggs at the start of the nesting season, which is around the beginning of spring.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RsrV3uOZKN4C|title=The Encyclopedia of Birds|last=Anonymous|date=2005|publisher=Parragon Publishing India|isbn=978-1-4054-9851-7|page=50|language=en}} At this time she is left by the male who joins up with other males to await the moulting period, which begins in June (in the Northern Hemisphere).{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=Sd9FAAAAYAAJ&q=mallard+moulting+period|title=Moult in birds|last1=Ginn|first1=H. B.|last2=Melville|first2=Dorothy Sutherland|date=1983|publisher=British Trust for Ornithology|isbn=978-0-903793-02-5|language=en}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4htx09cb-6gC&q=mallard++moulting+period+which+begins+in+June&pg=PA359|title=Waterbirds Around the World: A Global Overview of the Conservation, Management and Research of the World's Waterbird Flyways|last1=Boere|first1=G. C.|last2=Galbraith|first2=Colin A.|last3=Stroud|first3=David A.|date=2006|publisher=The Stationery Office|isbn=978-0-11-497333-9|language=en}} During the brief time before this, however, the males are still sexually potent and some of them either remain on standby to sire replacement [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]]es (for female mallards that have lost or abandoned their previous clutch){{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4htx09cb-6gC&q=mallard++moulting+period+which+begins+in+June&pg=PA359|title=Waterbirds Around the World: A Global Overview of the Conservation, Management and Research of the World's Waterbird Flyways|last1=Boere|first1=G. C.|last2=Galbraith|first2=Colin A.|last3=Stroud|first3=David A.|date=2006|publisher=The Stationery Office|isbn=978-0-11-497333-9|page=359|language=en}} or forcibly mate with females that appear to be isolated or unattached regardless of their species and whether or not they have a brood of ducklings.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=jUUKN77Ko_0C&q=mallard+forcibly+mate+with+females&pg=PA130|title=Field Guide to Urban Wildlife|last=Feinstein|first=Julie|date=2011|publisher=Stackpole Books|isbn=978-0-8117-0585-1|page=130|language=en}} File:Anas platyrhynchos MWNH 1048.JPG|Egg File:Mallard and ducklings - geograph.org.uk - 4473798.jpg|Young ducklings File:Mallard ducklings (3752889877).jpg|Older ducklings File:Duckling in Central Park (81320).jpg|Moulting File:Anas platyrhynchos juvenile JdP 2013-06-14 n01 retusche.jpg|Fledgling female and male File:Female Mallard IMG 1434.jpg|Adult female Nesting sites are typically on the ground, hidden in vegetation where the female's speckled plumage serves as effective [[camouflage]],{{cite web|date=2020-03-29|title=The secret life of mallard ducks|url=https://scottishwildlifetrust.org.uk/2020/03/the-secret-life-of-mallard-ducks/|access-date=2021-04-20|website=Scottish Wildlife Trust|language=en-GB}} but female mallards have also been known to nest in hollows in trees, boathouses, roof gardens and on balconies, sometimes resulting in hatched offspring having difficulty following their parent to water.{{cite web|title=Nesting mallards|url=https://www.rspb.org.uk/makeahomeforwildlife/advice/helpingbirdstest/nestingmallards/|access-date=2021-04-20|website=The RSPB|language=en-GB}} [[File:Duckling (47016759604).jpg|thumb|Duckling]] [[File:20250709 mallard duckling wethersfield cove PD208385 1.jpg|thumb|Swimming duckling]] [[File:Canard colvert femelle et ses petits (Lac Archambeault, Qc).webm|thumb|Female mallard with five ducklings]]Egg clutches number 8–13 creamy white to greenish-buff eggs free of speckles.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wWQw11SLvVwC&q=mallard+50+days+to+fledling&pg=PA1525|title=International Wildlife Encyclopedia: Leopard – marten|last1=Burton|first1=Maurice|last2=Burton|first2=Robert|date=2002|publisher=Marshall Cavendish|isbn=978-0-7614-7277-3|language=en|access-date=16 June 2016}}{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=evQvBAAAQBAJ|title=The Book of Eggs: A Life-Size Guide to the Eggs of Six Hundred of the World's Bird Species|last=Hauber|first=Mark E.|date=2014|publisher=University of Chicago Press|isbn=978-0-226-05781-1|page=83}} They measure about {{convert|58|mm|in|abbr=on}} in length and {{convert|32|mm|in|abbr=on}} in width. The eggs are laid on alternate days, and [[Incubation period|incubation]] begins when the clutch is almost complete. Incubation takes 27–28{{nbsp}}days and [[Fledge|fledging]] takes 50–60{{nbsp}}days.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=cYDOY6DszoIC&q=mallard+The+nesting+period+clutch+is+8%E2%80%9313+eggs&pg=PA131|title=The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Birds|last1=DK|last2=International|first2=BirdLife|date=1 March 2011|publisher=Dorling Kindersley Limited|isbn=978-1-4053-3616-1|language=en}} The ducklings are [[precocial]] and fully capable of swimming as soon as they hatch.{{cite news|url=https://audubonportland.org/our-work/rehabilitate-wildlife/having-a-wildlife-problem/urban-mallards/#:~:text=Mallard%20ducklings%20are%20precocial%2C%20meaning,own%20within%20minutes%20of%20hatching.&text=She%20will%20typically%20lead%20them,her%20for%2042%2D60%20days |title=Urban Mallards - Portland Audubon |newspaper=Portland Audubon |publisher=Audubonportland.org |date= |access-date=2022-03-16}} However, [[Imprinting (psychology)|filial imprinting]] compels them to instinctively stay near the mother, not only for warmth and protection but also to learn about and remember their habitat as well as how and where to forage for food.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=W9bBCwAAQBAJ&q=mallard+mother+warmth+protection+forage+food&pg=PA51|title=British Columbia: Graced by Nature's Palette|last=Townsley|first=Frank|date=10 March 2016|publisher=FriesenPress|isbn=978-1-4602-7773-7|language=en}} Though adoptions are known to occur, female mallards typically do not tolerate stray ducklings near their broods, and will violently attack and drive away any unfamiliar young, sometimes going as far as to kill them.{{cite web|title=Mallard Ducklings {{!}} Nesting Ducks|url=https://www.rspb.org.uk/birds-and-wildlife/advice/how-you-can-help-birds/where-do-ducks-nest/mallard-ducklings/|access-date=2021-10-09|website=The RSPB|language=en-GB}} When ducklings mature into flight-capable juveniles, they learn about and remember their traditional migratory routes (unless they are born and raised in captivity). In New Zealand, where mallards are [[Naturalisation (biology)|naturalised]], the nesting season has been found to be longer; eggs and clutches are larger and nest survival is generally greater compared with mallards in their native range.{{cite journal |author1= Sheppard, J.L. |author2= Amundson, C.L. | author3= Arnold, T.W. | author4= Klee, D. | year=2019| title= Nesting ecology of a naturalized population of Mallards Anas platyrhynchos in New Zealand| journal=Ibis| volume=161| pages= 504–520| doi= 10.1111/ibi.12656| issue=3|bibcode= 2019Ibis..161..504S |s2cid= 91988206 }} In cases where a nest or brood fails, some mallards may mate for a second time in an attempt to raise a second clutch, typically around early-to-mid summer. In addition, mallards may occasionally breed during the autumn in cases of unseasonably warm weather; one such instance of a 'late' clutch occurred in November 2011, in which a female successfully hatched and raised a clutch of eleven ducklings at the [[WWT London Wetland Centre|London Wetland Centre]].{{Cite news|date=2011-11-20|title=Ducklings hatch at London Wetland Centre|language=en-GB|work=BBC News|url=https://www.bbc.com/news/uk-england-london-15809760|access-date=2021-05-24}} During the breeding season, both male and female mallards can become aggressive, driving off competitors to themselves or their mate by charging at them.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=c3brsyiAJ0wC&q=mallard+breeding+season+male+female+mallards+aggressive&pg=PA66|title=Man and Wildfowl|last=Kear|first=Janet|date=30 November 2010|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4081-3760-4|language=en}} Males tend to fight more than females and attack each other by repeatedly pecking at their rival's chest, ripping out feathers and even skin on rare occasions. Female mallards are also known to carry out 'inciting displays', which encourage other ducks in the flock to begin fighting.{{Cite journal|last=Cunningham|first=Emma J. A.|date=2003-05-01|title=Female mate preferences and subsequent resistance to copulation in the mallard|url=https://academic.oup.com/beheco/article/14/3/326/257579|journal=Behavioral Ecology|language=en|volume=14|issue=3|pages=326–333|doi=10.1093/beheco/14.3.326|issn=1045-2249|doi-access=free}} It is possible that this behaviour allows the female to evaluate the strength of potential partners.{{cite web|url=https://web.stanford.edu/group/stanfordbirds/text/essays/Duck_Displays.html|title=Duck Displays|website=web.stanford.edu|access-date=2020-02-22}} The drakes that end up being left out after the others have paired off with mating partners sometimes target an isolated female duck, even one of a different species, and proceed to chase and peck at her until she weakens, at which point the males take turns copulating with the female.{{Cite journal|last=Moeliker|first=Cornelis|date=2001|title=The first case of homosexual necrophilia in the mallard Anas platyrhynchos (Aves:Anatidae)|url=http://www.hetnatuurhistorisch.nl/fileadmin/user_upload/documents-nmr/Persberichten/Persberichten/persberichten_2013/DSA8_243-248.pdf|journal=Deinsea 8|pages=243–248}} Lebret (1961) calls this behaviour "Attempted Rape Flight", and [[Stanley Cramp]] and K.E.L. Simmons (1977) speak of "rape-intent flights". Male mallards also occasionally chase other male ducks of a different species, and even each other, in the same way. In one documented case of "homosexual necrophilia", a male mallard copulated with another male he was chasing after the chased male died upon flying into a glass window. This paper was awarded an [[Ig Nobel Prize]] in 2003.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wCrxAAAAMAAJ|title=Annals of Improbable Research|date=2005|publisher=MIT Museum|language=en}} Mallards are opportunistically targeted by [[brood parasite]]s, occasionally having eggs laid in their nests by [[Redhead (duck)|redhead]]s, [[ruddy duck]]s, [[lesser scaup]], [[gadwall]]s, [[northern shoveller]]s, [[northern pintail]]s, [[cinnamon teal]], [[common goldeneye]]s, and other mallards.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=wvpPMwEACAAJ|title=Ducks, Geese, and Swans of North America|last=Baldassarre|first=Guy A.|date=2014|publisher=JHU Press|isbn=978-1-4214-0751-7|language=en}} These eggs are generally accepted when they resemble the eggs of the host mallard, but the hen may attempt to eject them or even abandon the nest if parasitism occurs during egg laying. ==Predators and threats== [[File:Mute Swan attacking Mallard Family (7617017146).jpg|thumb|A male [[mute swan]] (''Cygnus olor'') driving off a female mallard.]] In addition to human hunting, mallards of all ages (but especially young ones) and in all locations must contend with a wide diversity of predators including [[Bird of prey|raptor]]s and [[owl]]s, [[Mustelidae|mustelid]]s, [[Corvidae|corvid]]s, [[snake]]s, [[raccoon]]s, [[opossum]]s, [[skunk]]s, [[turtle]]s, large [[fish]], [[Felidae|felid]]s, and [[Canidae|canid]]s, the last two including domestic cats and dogs.{{Cite journal|last=Martz|first=Gerald F.|date=1967|title=Effects of nesting cover removal on breeding puddle ducks|journal=Journal of Wildlife Management|volume=31|issue=2|pages=236–247|doi=10.2307/3798312|jstor=3798312}} The most prolific natural predators of adult mallards are [[red fox]]es (''Vulpes vulpes''; which most often pick off brooding females) and the faster or larger birds of prey, (e.g. [[peregrine falcon]]s, ''[[Aquila (genus)|Aquila]]'' or ''[[Haliaeetus]]'' eagles). In North America, adult mallards face no fewer than 15 species of birds of prey, from [[northern harrier]]s (''Circus hudsonius'') and [[short-eared owl]]s (''Asio flammeus'') (both smaller than a mallard) to huge [[Bald eagle|bald]] (''Haliaeetus leucocephalus'') and [[golden eagle]]s (''Aquila chrysaetos''), and about a dozen species of mammalian predators, not counting several more avian and mammalian predators who threaten eggs and nestlings. Mallards are also preyed upon by other waterside [[apex predator]]s, such as [[grey heron]]s (''Ardea cinerea''),{{Cite journal|last1=Marquiss|first1=M.|last2=Leitch|first2=A. F.|date=1 October 1990|title=The diet of Grey Herons Ardea cinerea breeding at Loch Leven, Scotland, and the importance of their predation on ducklings|journal=Ibis|language=en|volume=132|issue=4|pages=535–549|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1990.tb00277.x|issn=1474-919X}} [[great blue heron]]s (''Ardea herodias'') and [[black-crowned night heron]]s (''Nycticorax nycticorax''), the [[European herring gull]] (''Larus argentatus''), the [[wels catfish]] (''Silurus glanis''), and the [[northern pike]] (''Esox lucius'').{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=X1l0cl6_DzAC&q=mallard+preyed+Northern+pike|title=Reader's Digest Scenic wonders of Canada: an illustrated guide to our natural splendors|date=1976|publisher=Reader's Digest Association (Canada)|isbn=978-0-88850-049-6|language=en}} Crows (''[[Corvus (genus)|Corvus]]''{{nbsp}}{{Abbr|spp.|species}}) are also known to kill ducklings and adults on occasion.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=CuX1f28Kg-EC&q=crows+eating+mallards&pg=PA140|title=Ecosystem Matters: Activity and Resource Guide for Environmental Educators|last=Adams|first=Mary|date=October 1995|publisher=DIANE Publishing|isbn=978-0-7881-2453-2|language=en}} Also, mallards may be attacked by larger anseriformes such as [[swan]]s (''[[Cygnus (genus)|Cygnus]]''{{nbsp}}{{Abbr|spp.|species}}) and [[geese]] during the breeding season, and are frequently driven off by these birds over territorial disputes. [[Mute swan]]s (''Cygnus olor'') have been known to attack or even kill mallards if they feel that the ducks pose a threat to their offspring.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sBfSBAAAQBAJ&q=mallard+Mute+swans+drown+offspring&pg=PA52|title=The Birds of Leicestershire and Rutland|last1=Fray|first1=Rob|last2=Davies|first2=Roger|last3=Gamble|first3=Dave|last4=Harrop|first4=Andrew|last5=Lister|first5=Steve|date=30 June 2010|publisher=Bloomsbury Publishing|isbn=978-1-4081-3311-8|language=en}} [[Common loon]]s (''Gavia inmer'') are similarly territorial and aggressive towards other birds in such disputes, and will frequently drive mallards away from their territory.{{cite web|last=Sperry|first=Mark L.|title=Common Loon Attacks on Waterfowl|url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jfo/v058n02/p0201-p0205.pdf|publisher=Minnesota Department of Natural Resources, Wetland Wildlife Populations and Research Group}} However, in 2019, a pair of common loons in Wisconsin were observed raising a mallard duckling for several weeks, having seemingly adopted the bird after it had been abandoned by its parents.{{cite web|date=2019-07-12|title=A Mallard Duckling Is Thriving—and Maybe Diving—Under the Care of Loon Parents|url=https://www.audubon.org/news/a-mallard-duckling-thriving-and-maybe-diving-under-care-loon-parents|access-date=2021-10-09|website=Audubon|language=en}} In summer, a combination of hot temperatures and reduced water levels place mallards at an increased risk of contracting [[botulism]], as these conditions are ideal for ''[[Clostridium botulinum]]'' to propagate, with the birds also more likely to come into contact with [[botulinum toxin]] produced by the bacteria. Outbreaks of botulism among mallard populations can lead to mass die-offs.{{Cite web |title=Mallard |url=https://wildlifeillinois.org/identify-wildlife/mallard/ |access-date=2024-06-13 |website=Wildlife Illinois |language=en-US}} The predation-avoidance behaviour of sleeping with one eye open, allowing one brain hemisphere to remain aware while the other half sleeps, was first demonstrated in mallards, although it is believed to be widespread among birds in general.{{Cite journal|last=Niels C.|first=Rattenborg|date=1999|title=Half-awake to the risk of predation|journal=Nature|volume=397|issue=6718|pages=397–398|doi=10.1038/17037|pmid=29667967|bibcode=1999Natur.397..397R|s2cid=4427166}} == Status and conservation == [[File:Mallards pond 2009.ogv|thumb|thumbtime=1|Several drakes swimming in a pond]] [[File:Mallard - Gräsand (Anas platyrhynchos) - Ystad-2024.ogg|thumb|A mallard (male) eats rolled oats from the hand.]] Since 1998, the mallard has been rated as a species of [[least concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List of Endangered Species]]. This is because it has a large range–more than {{cvt|65000000|km2}}, though its very large population – estimated at more than 17 million mature individuals as of 2025 – is decreasing. Unlike many waterfowl, mallards have benefited from human alterations to the world{{snd}}so much so that they are now considered an [[invasive species]] in some regions.{{Cite journal|last1=Mooney|first1=H. A.|last2=Cleland|first2=E. E.|date=8 May 2001|title=The evolutionary impact of invasive species|journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences|language=en|volume=98|issue=10|pages=5446–5451|doi=10.1073/pnas.091093398|issn=0027-8424|pmc=33232|pmid=11344292|bibcode=2001PNAS...98.5446M|doi-access=free}} They are a common sight in urban parks, lakes, ponds, and other human-made water features in the regions they inhabit, and are often tolerated or encouraged in human habitat due to their placid nature towards humans, their ducklings and their beautiful and iridescent colours. While most are not domesticated, mallards are so successful at coexisting in human regions that the main conservation risk they pose comes from the loss of genetic diversity among a region's traditional ducks once humans and mallards colonise an area. Mallards are very adaptable, being able to live and even thrive in urban areas which may have supported more localised, sensitive species of waterfowl before development.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sH0KH-JLlmkC&q=Mallard+very+adaptable+able+to+live+in+urban+areas|title=A Guide to Urban Wildlife Management|last1=Leedy|first1=Daniel L.|last2=Adams|first2=Lowell W.|date=1984|publisher=National Institute for Urban Wildlife|language=en}} The release of [[Feral animal|feral]] mallards in areas where they are not native sometimes creates problems through interbreeding with indigenous [[waterfowl]]. These non-migratory mallards interbreed with indigenous wild ducks from local populations of closely related species through [[genetic pollution]] by producing fertile offspring. Complete hybridisation of various species of wild duck gene pools could result in the extinction of many indigenous waterfowl.{{cite web |url=https://pubs.usgs.gov/fs/2007/3047/fs2007-3047.pdf |title=Hawaiian Duck's Future Threatened by Feral Mallards |last1=Uyehara |first1=Kimberly |last2=Engilis |first2=Andrew |last3=Reynolds |first3=Michelle |editor-last=Hendley |editor-first=James |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170504072645/https://pubs.usgs.gov/fs/2007/3047/fs2007-3047.pdf |archive-date=4 May 2017 |access-date=19 June 2017}} The mallard itself is the ancestor of most domestic ducks, and its naturally evolved wild gene pool gets genetically polluted in turn by the domestic and feral populations.{{cite web|url=https://www.eagletimes.com/lifestyles/of-a-feather-why-this-duck/article_8f380da9-88be-5d03-a423-40815a5b7270.html|title=Of a Feather: Why this duck?|website=The Eagle Times|date=23 October 2021 }} Over time, a continuum of hybrids ranging between almost typical examples of either species develop; the [[speciation]] process is beginning to reverse itself. This has created [[Conservation biology|conservation]] concerns for relatives of the mallard, such as the [[Hawaiian duck]], the New Zealand grey duck (''A.{{nbsp}}s. superciliosa)'' subspecies of the [[Pacific black duck]], the American black duck, the [[mottled duck]], [[Meller's duck]], the [[yellow-billed duck]], and the [[Mexican duck]], in the latter case even leading to a dispute as to whether these birds should be considered a species (and thus entitled to more conservation research and funding) or included in the mallard species. Ecological changes and hunting have also led to a decline of local species; for example, the New Zealand grey duck population declined drastically due to overhunting in the mid-20th century. Hybrid offspring of Hawaiian ducks seem to be less well adapted to native habitat, and using them in re-introduction projects apparently reduces success. In summary, the problems of mallards "hybridising away" relatives is more a consequence of local ducks declining than of mallards spreading; [[allopatric speciation]] and isolating behaviour have produced today's diversity of mallard-like ducks despite the fact that, in most, if not all, of these populations, hybridisation must have occurred to some extent.{{Cite journal |last1=Tubaro |first1=Pablo L. |last2=Lijtmaer |first2=Dario A. |date=1 October 2002 |title=Hybridization patterns and the evolution of reproductive isolation in ducks |journal=Biological Journal of the Linnean Society |language=en |volume=77 |issue=2 |pages=193–200 |doi=10.1046/j.1095-8312.2002.00096.x |issn=0024-4066|doi-access=free |hdl=11336/136485 |hdl-access=free }} File:Wood Duck × Mallard imported 3 December 2024 (cropped).jpg|''x [[Eastern spot-billed duck|A. zonorhyncha]]'' === Invasiveness === [[File:Anas platyrhynchos oustaleti last male.jpg|thumb|The last male [[Mariana mallard]]]] Mallards are causing severe "genetic pollution" to South Africa's [[biodiversity]] by breeding with endemic ducks even though the ''[[Agreement on the Conservation of African-Eurasian Migratory Waterbirds]] – ''an agreement to protect the local waterfowl populations – applies to the mallard as well as other ducks.{{cite web |url=https://pca-cpa.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/175/2016/11/Agreement-on-the-Conservation-of-African-Eurasian-Migratory-Waterbirds.pdf|title=Agreement on the Conservation of African-Eurasian Migratory Waterbirds |website=Official Journal of the European Union |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170619111955/https://pca-cpa.org/wp-content/uploads/sites/175/2016/11/Agreement-on-the-Conservation-of-African-Eurasian-Migratory-Waterbirds.pdf |archive-date=19 June 2017 |access-date=19 June 2017}} The hybrids of mallards and the yellow-billed duck are fertile, capable of producing hybrid offspring.{{cite web |url=http://www.mdga.co.za/code/environment/feb2011.html |title=Marina da Gama |date=2007 |website=www.mdga.co.za |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170211171037/http://mdga.co.za/code/environment/feb2011.html|archive-date=11 February 2017 |access-date=19 June 2017}} If this continues, only hybrids occur and in the long term result in the extinction of various indigenous waterfowl. The mallard can crossbreed with 63 other species, posing a severe threat to indigenous waterfowl's genetic integrity.{{Cite news |url=http://sanquentinnews.com/those-mighty-mallards-can-bust-the-speed-limit/ |title=Those mighty mallards can bust the speed limit |last=Marsh |first=David |date=1 June 2010 |work=San Quentin News |access-date=19 June 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170615200616/http://sanquentinnews.com/those-mighty-mallards-can-bust-the-speed-limit/ |archive-date=15 June 2017 |language=en-US}} Mallards and their hybrids compete with indigenous birds for resources, including nest sites, roosting sites, and food. Availability of mallards, mallard ducklings, and fertilised mallard eggs for public sale and private ownership, either as poultry or as pets, is currently legal in the United States, except for the state of [[Florida]], which has currently banned domestic ownership of mallards. This is to prevent hybridisation with the native [[mottled duck]]. The mallard is considered an invasive species in Australia and New Zealand,{{r|cramp|page=505}} where it competes with the [[Pacific black duck]] (known as the grey duck locally in New Zealand) which was over-hunted in the past. There, and elsewhere, mallards are spreading with increasing [[urbanisation]] and hybridising with local relatives. The eastern or [[Chinese spot-billed duck]] is currently introgressing into the mallard populations of the [[Primorsky Krai]], possibly due to [[Habitat (ecology)|habitat]] changes from [[global warming]]. The Mariana mallard was a resident [[allopatric]] population – in most respects a good species – apparently initially derived from mallard–Pacific black duck hybrids; it became [[extinct]] in the late 20th century.{{Cite book |title=Wildfowl |last1=Madge |first1=Steve |author-link1=Steve Madge |last2=Burn |first2=Hilary |author-link2=Hilary Burn |year=2010 |orig-date=1989 |publisher=Christopher Helm Publications |isbn=978-1-4081-3895-3 |page=211 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=gFawO8q9VfIC&pg=PA211}} The [[Laysan duck]] is an insular relative of the mallard, with a very small and fluctuating population.{{cite journal |last1=Browne |first1=Robert |last2=Griffin |first2=Curtice |last3=Chang |first3=Paul |last4=Hubley |first4=Mark |last5=Martin |first5=Amy |date=1993 |title=Genetic Divergence Among Populations of the Hawaiian Duck, Laysan Duck, and Mallard |journal=The Auk |volume=110 |issue=1 |pages=49–56 |jstor=4088230}} Mallards sometimes arrive on its island home during migration, and can be expected to occasionally have remained and hybridised with Laysan ducks as long as these species have existed.{{cite web |url=https://www.fws.gov/pacific/ecoservices/documents/LaysanDuckRRP/LaysanDuckRRPChap2.html |title=Recovery Strategy – Laysan Duck Revised Recovery Plan |date=September 2009 |website=www.fws.gov |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170620234655/https://www.fws.gov/pacific/ecoservices/documents/LaysanDuckRRP/LaysanDuckRRPChap2.html |archive-date=20 June 2017 |access-date=19 June 2017}} However, these hybrids are less well adapted to the peculiar ecological conditions of [[Laysan Island]] than the local ducks, and thus have lower [[Fitness (biology)|fitness]]. Laysan ducks were found throughout the Hawaiian archipelago before 400{{nbsp}}AD, after which they suffered a rapid decline during the Polynesian colonisation.{{cite web |url=http://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Anas_laysanensis/ |title=Anas laysanensis (Laysan duck) |last1=Myers |first1=P. |last2=Espinosa |first2=R. |date=2016 |website=Animal Diversity Web |language=en |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160313053719/http://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Anas_laysanensis/ |archive-date=13 March 2016 |access-date=19 June 2017 |last3=Parr |first3=C. S. |last4=Jones |first4=T. |last5=Hammond |first5=G. S. |last6=Dewey |first6=T. A.}} Now, their range includes only Laysan Island. It is one of the successfully translocated birds, after having become nearly extinct in the early 20th century.{{cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RttCNJzMUEEC&q=laysan+duck+translocated&pg=PA8 |article=Clostridium Infections |title=Advances in Research and Treatment |edition=2011 |date=9 January 2012 |publisher=ScholarlyEditions |isbn=978-1-4649-6013-0 |language=en}} == Relationship with humans == {{further|Domestic duck}} === Domestication === [[File:Anas platyrhynchos domesticus 001.jpg|thumb|An [[American Pekin|American Pekin duck]], a breed of [[domestic duck]] derived from the mallard]] Mallards have often been ubiquitous in their regions among the ponds, rivers, and streams of human parks, farms, and other human-made waterways{{snd}}even to the point of visiting water features in human [[courtyard]]s.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=kfgyAQAAMAAJ&q=mallard+rivers+ponds&pg=PA118-IA19|title=Channel Improvements, Columbia and Lower Willamette River Federal Navigation Channel, (OR, WA): Environmental Impact Statement|date=1999}} Mallards have had a long relationship with humans. Almost all [[domestic duck]] breeds derive from the mallard, with the exception of a few [[Muscovy duck|Muscovy]] breeds,{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hW6BCwAAQBAJ&pg=PA9|title=Poultry Behaviour and Welfare|last1=Appleby|first1=Michael C.|last2=Mench|first2=Joy A.|last3=Hughes|first3=Barry O.|date=2004|publisher=CABI|isbn=978-0-85199-667-7}} and are listed under the [[Trinomial nomenclature|trinomial name]] ''A. p. domesticus''. Mallards are generally [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]] while domestic ducks are mostly [[Animal sexual behaviour#Polygamy|polygamous]]. Domestic ducks have no territorial behaviour and are less aggressive than mallards.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=AQ89AAAAIAAJ&pg=PA407|title=The Agrarian History of England and Wales: Volume 1, Part 1, Prehistory|last1=Piggott|first1=Stuart|last2=Thirsk|first2=Joan|date=2 April 1981|publisher=CUP Archive|isbn=978-0-521-08741-4}} Domestic ducks are mostly kept for meat; their eggs are also eaten, and have a strong flavour. They were first domesticated in [[Southeast Asia]] at least 4,000 years ago, during the [[Neolithic|Neolithic Age]], and were also farmed by the [[Demography of the Roman Empire|Roman]]s in Europe, and the [[Malays (ethnic group)|Malays]] in [[Asia]].{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=MfrdBcKd79wC&pg=PA7|title=Ducks, Geese and Swans: General chapters, species accounts (Anhima to Salvadorina) |last=Kear|first=Janet |date=2005 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=978-0-198-61008-3 }} As the domestic duck and the mallard are the same species as each other, it is common for mallards to mate with domestic ducks and produce hybrid offspring that are fully fertile.{{Cite book |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xtEGCAAAQBAJ&pg=PT212|title=Elements of Ethology: A textbook for agricultural and veterinary students|last=Wood-Gush|first=D.|date=6 December 2012|publisher=Springer Science & Business Media |isbn=978-94-009-5931-6}} Because of this, mallards have been found to contain genes of the domestic duck. While the keeping of domestic breeds is more popular, pure-bred mallards are sometimes kept for eggs and meat,{{cite web|url=http://duckhobby.com/raising-mallard-ducks-how-to-raise-mallards-in-your-backyard-duck-yard/|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180216113254/http://duckhobby.com/raising-mallard-ducks-how-to-raise-mallards-in-your-backyard-duck-yard/|archive-date=16 February 2018|title=Raising Mallard Ducks: How to Raise Mallards In Your Backyard Duck Yard – DuckHobby.com|language=en-US|access-date=2019-05-03}} although they may require wing clipping to restrict flying. === Hunting === {{further|Waterfowl hunting}} [[File:Nature_Mort_-_Hen_and_Drake_Mallard.jpg|thumb|[[George Hetzel]], mallard [[still life]] painting, 1883–1884]] Mallards are one of the most common [[species]] shot in [[waterfowl hunting]] due to their large [[Population (biology)|population]] size. The ideal location for hunting mallards is considered to be where the water level is somewhat shallow where the birds can be found foraging for food.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=cmFClEqEY0kC&pg=PA108|title=Field & Stream|last=O'Neill|first=Michael J.|date=February 1973|publisher=Columbia Broadcasting System Publications|volume=77|page=108|issue=10}} Hunting mallards might cause the population to decline in some places, at some times, and with some populations.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=nTM3AQAAMAAJ&pg=PA90|title=Cape Cod National Seashore (N.S.), Hunting Program: Environmental Impact Statement|date=2007|page=90}} In certain countries, the mallard may be legally shot but is protected under national acts and policies. For example, in the United Kingdom, the mallard is protected under the [[Wildlife and Countryside Act 1981]], which restricts certain hunting methods or taking or killing mallards.{{Cite book|title=Tips for Pheasant Shooting from some of the Finest Hunters|last=Walsingham|first=Lord|publisher=Read Books Ltd|year=2016|isbn=978-1-4733-5705-1}} === Mallard–vehicle collisions === {{Further|Bird strike}} Since standardized data collection began in 1990, the United States [[Federal Aviation Administration]] has recorded 1320 mallard collisions with aircraft, 261 of which caused damage to the craft (through 2022).{{Cite book |last1=Dolbeer |first1=Richard A. |url=https://www.faa.gov/sites/faa.gov/files/Wildlife-Strike-Report-1990-2022.pdf |title=Wildlife Strikes to Civil Aircraft in the United States, 1990 - 2022 |last2=Begier |first2=Michael J. |last3=Miller |first3=Phyllis R. |last4=Weller |first4=John R. |last5=Anderson |first5=Amy L. |publisher=Federal Aviation Administration |year=2023 |edition=29th |location=Washington, D.C., USA |page=55}} In the United States, the mallard ranks as the 7th most hazardous bird to both military and commercial aircraft.{{Cite journal |last1=Pfeiffer |first1=Morgan B. |last2=Blackwell |first2=Bradley F. |last3=DeVault |first3=Travis L. |date=2018 |title=Quantification of avian hazards to military aircraft and implications for wildlife management |journal=PLOS ONE |volume=13 |issue=11 |article-number=e0206599 |doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0206599|doi-access=free |pmid=30383828 |pmc=6211720 |bibcode=2018PLoSO..1306599P }}{{Cite journal |last1=DeVault |first1=Travis L. |last2=Blackwell |first2=Bradley F. |last3=Seamans |first3=Thomas W. |last4=Begier |first4=Michael J. |last5=Kougher |first5=Jason D. |last6=Washburn |first6=Jenny E. |last7=Miller |first7=Phyllis R. |last8=Dolbeer |first8=Richard A. |date=2018 |title=Estimating interspecific economic risk of bird strikes with aircraft |journal=Wildlife Society Bulletin |volume=42 |issue=1 |pages=94–101 |doi=10.1002/wsb.859|bibcode=2018WSBu...42...94D }} Mallards are of particular concern due to their ubiquity; they are widespread and adaptable to urban environments.{{Cite journal |last1=Figley |first1=William K. |last2=VanDruff |first2=Larry W. |date=1982 |title=The ecology of urban mallards |journal=Wildlife Monographs |volume=81 |issue=81 |pages=3–39|jstor=3830665 }}{{Cite journal |last1=Guenin |first1=Shane |last2=Pakula |first2=Carson J. |last3=Skaggs |first3=Jonathon |last4=Fernandez-Juricic |first4=Esteban |last5=DeVault |first5=Travis |date=2024 |title=Inefficacy of mallard flight responses to approaching vehicles |journal=PeerJ |volume=12 |article-number=e18124 |doi=10.7717/peerj.18124|doi-access=free |pmid=39346053 |pmc=11438428 }} Mallards also generally fail to avoid approaching vehicles in experimental settings, especially at high vehicle speeds. Though most [[bird strike]]s occur during the takeoff and landing phases of flight, at least one mallard has been struck at cruising altitude (21,000 feet).{{Cite journal |last=Manville |first=Richard H. |date=1963 |title=Altitude record for mallard |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v075n01/p0092-p0092.pdf |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=75 |issue=1 |page=92}} === As food === Since ancient times, the mallard has been eaten as food. The wild mallard was eaten in [[Neolithic Greece]].{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=KdR4jRJCxEsC&pg=PA124|title=Food in the Ancient World from A to Z|last=Dalby|first=Andrew|date=15 April 2013|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-95422-2}} Usually, only the breast and thigh meat is eaten.{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=TOG-ItIHp_kC&pg=PA584|title=The Visual Food Encyclopedia|date=1996|publisher=Québec Amerique|isbn=978-2-7644-0898-8}} It does not need to be [[Hanging (meat)|hung]] before preparation, and is often braised or roasted, sometimes flavoured with [[bitter orange]] or with [[Port (wine)|port]].{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=pZ-1AQAAQBAJ&pg=PT1722|title=The Oxford Companion to Food|last=Davidson|first=Alan|date=2006|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-101825-1|page=472|language=en}} === In culture === [[File:Ferdinand von Wright - Mallards - A-2002-608 - Finnish National Gallery.jpg|thumb|right|''Mallards'' (1874) by [[Ferdinand von Wright]]]] ''[[Make Way for Ducklings]]'' is a [[Children's book|children's picture book]] written and illustrated by [[Robert McCloskey]]. The book centers on a pair of mallards who raise their ducklings in the [[Boston Public Garden]].{{Cite book |title=Make way for ducklings |date=1988 |publisher=The Viking Press |isbn=978-0-670-45149-4 |editor-last=McCloskey |editor-first=Robert |edition=45. [pr.]}} ''[[Migration (2023 film)|Migration]]'' is an animated [[Adventure film|adventure]] [[comedy film]] produced by [[Universal Pictures]] and [[Illumination (company)|Illumination]]. The story follows a family of mallards{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26 |title=Migration |url=https://www.uphe.com/movies/migration |access-date=2024-07-30 |publisher=uphe.com}} that live in a pond in [[New England]] and go on vacation in [[Jamaica]].{{Cite web|date=21 December 2023 |title=Illumination's Migration set to release in theatres |url=https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/news/international/us/illuminations-migration-set-to-release-in-theatres-when-will-it-stream-on-netflix/articleshow/106188597.cms?from=mdr |access-date=2026-02-14 |publisher=The Economic Times}} [[Trevor (duck)|The world's loneliest duck]], named "Trevor" by locals after New Zealand politician [[Trevor Mallard]], appeared without explanation on the Pacific island of [[Niue]], dying there in 2019. == Footnotes == {{noteslist}} == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{cite book |author=American Ornithologists' Union |author-link=American Ornithologists' Union |year=1983 |title=Check-list of North American Birds |edition=6th |publisher=American Ornithologists' Union}} {{cite web |website=audubon.org |publisher=National Audubon Society |url=http://web4.audubon.org/bird/BoA/F39_G4c.html |title=Brewer's Duck |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140513043906/http://web4.audubon.org/bird/BoA/F39_G4c.html |archive-date=13 May 2014 |access-date=19 May 2012}} {{cite journal |last1=Avise |first1=John C. |last2=Ankney |first2=C. 绿头鸭（学名：Anas platyrhynchos）又名大头绿（雄）、蒲鸭（雌）、野鴨，古稱鳧，是一種水鴨，在溫帶和亞熱帶的美洲、歐亞大陸和北非繁殖。它被引進到紐西蘭、澳大利亞、秘魯、巴西、烏拉圭、阿根廷、智利、哥倫比亞、福克蘭群島和南非。這種鴨屬於鴨亞科的雁形目鴨科。雄鴨（公鴨）有綠色頭部，而雌鴨（母鴨）主要有棕色斑點羽毛。兩性翅膀上都有一片稱為翼鏡羽的白邊黑色或虹彩紫色或藍色羽毛區域；雄鴨尤其傾向於有藍色翼鏡羽。
綠頭鴨長50—65 cm（20—26英寸），其中身體占約三分之二的長度。翼展為81—98 cm（32—39英寸），喙長為4.4至6.1 cm（1.7至2.4英寸）。它通常比大多數其他水鴨稍重，重達0.7—1.6公斤（1.5—3.5磅）。綠頭鴨生活在濕地，食用水生植物和小動物，是社會性動物，喜歡成群或大小不一的群體聚集。
雌鴨每隔一天產下8到13枚奶油白色到綠色無斑點的鳥蛋。孵化需時27到28天，出巢需時50到60天。鴨雛是早熟鳥，一孵化就能下水游泳。
國際自然保護聯盟（IUCN）認為綠頭鴨是一種無危物種。與許多水禽不同，綠頭鴨在某些地區被認為是入侵物種。這是一個非常適應力強的物種，能夠在城市地區生活甚至繁榮，這些地區可能曾支持過更多本地化、對環境敏感的水禽物種。非遷徙的綠頭鴨通過基因污染與本土野鴨雜交，產生可育的後代。各種野鴨基因庫的完全雜交可能導致許多本土水禽的滅絕。這個物種是大多數家鴨品種的主要祖先，其自然演化的野生基因庫已被家養和野生綠頭鴨種群的基因污染。


分類及演化歷史

綠頭鴨是1758年卡爾·林奈在《自然系統》第10 版中最初描述的鳥類物種之一。他給了它兩個雙名法名稱：Anas platyrhynchos和Anas boschas。後者直到1906年都被普遍接受，當時埃納爾·倫伯格確定A. platyrhynchos具有優先權，因為它出現在文本的前一頁。 科學名稱來自拉丁語Anas，意為“鴨”，以及古希臘語 πλατυρυγχος，platyrhynchus，意為“寬喙”（來自 πλατύς，platys，意為“寬”和 ρυγχός，rhunkhos，意為“喙”）。 Anas platyrhynchos的基因組於2013年定序。
名稱mallard最初指任何野生雄鴨，有時仍以此方式使用。 它來自古法語malart或mallart，意為“野生雄鴨”，雖然其真實來源不明。 它可能與古高地德語的男性專有名詞Madelhart有關，或至少受其影響，線索在替代的英語形式“maudelard”和“mawdelard”中。 也有提出Masle（雄性）一詞也有影響。
綠頭鴨經常與其在屬中的近親，如綠嘴黑鴨，以及與其遠緣的物種，如針尾鴨雜交，產生各種可能完全可繁殖的雜交種。 綠頭鴨在野外與超過40種物種雜交，在圈養中與另外20種物種雜交， 雖然可育的雜交種通常有兩個Anas父母。 野生綠頭鴨和其家養的同種具完全生殖能力；北美的許多野生綠頭鴨種群含有大量的家鴨DNA。

基因分析顯示，一些綠頭鴨似乎與其印太地區的親屬更接近，而另一些則與其美洲的親屬有關。 線粒體DNA的D環序列數據表明，綠頭鴨可能是在西伯利亞地區演化的。綠頭鴨骨骼突然出現在古代人類的食物遺跡和歐洲其他的化石骨骼堆積中，沒有明顯的當地前任物種。 在更新世期間，至少在歐洲和西亞的綠頭鴨大冰期年代物種被命名為Anas platyrhynchos palaeoboschas。 綠頭鴨的線粒體DNA在北美和歐亞種群之間存在區別， 但核基因組顯示出顯著缺乏基因結構。 在白令海周圍的綠頭鴨中，可以找到典型的美洲綠頭鴨親屬和東方斑嘴鴨的單倍型。 阿留申群島擁有一群綠頭鴨，它們似乎正在進化成為一個亞種，因為與其他種群的基因交流非常有限。
此外，舊世界綠頭鴨和新大陸綠頭鴨之間缺乏顯著的形態學差異，這顯示了它們基因組之間的高度共享，使得像中國斑嘴鴨這樣的鳥類與舊世界綠頭鴨高度相似，而像夏威夷鴨這樣的鳥類則與新大陸綠頭鴨高度相似。
綠頭鴨的大小有地域性；例如，來自格陵蘭的個體雖然較大，但喙較小，羽毛顏色較淺，身體較粗壯，曾被分類為一個獨立的亞種，即格陵蘭綠頭鴨（A. p. conboschas）。


描述

綠頭鴨是一種中等大小的水禽，通常比大多數其他水鴨稍重。它長50—65 cm（20—26英寸） – 其中身體占約三分之二 – 翼展為81—98 cm（32—39英寸），重0.7—1.6公斤（1.5—3.5磅） 在標準測量中，翼弦長25.7至30.6 cm（10.1至12.0英寸），喙長4.4至6.1 cm（1.7至2.4英寸），蹠骨長4.1至4.8 cm（1.6至1.9英寸）。
繁殖期的雄性綠頭鴨易於辨認，擁有光亮的深綠色頭部和一條白色的頸圈，將頭部與紫色帶棕色的胸部、灰棕色的翅膀和淺灰色的腹部分開。 雄性的尾部是黑色的，並有白色邊緣的深色尾羽。 雄鴨的喙呈黃色橙色，頂端有黑色，而雌鴨的喙通常較暗，從黑色到斑駁的橙色和棕色不等。 雌性綠頭鴨外觀顏色斑駁，每根羽毛都呈現出從淺黃色到深棕色的鮮明對比，大多數雌性水鴨都是這種顏色，並且有淺黃色的臉頰、眉毛、喉嚨和頸部，與較暗的頭頂和眼紋形成對比。 綠頭鴨像其他兩性異形的鳥類一樣，有時會經歷自發的性別逆轉， 通常是由於性器官，如雌鴨的卵巢受損或功能失調所引起。 這種現象可能會導致雌性綠頭鴨呈現出雄性羽毛，反之亦然（表型女性化或男性化）。

雄性和雌性綠頭鴨都有明顯的紫藍色彩虹翼鏡羽，邊緣帶白色，在飛行或休息時很顯眼，但在夏季的年度換羽期間會暫時脫落。 剛孵化時，鴨雛的羽毛在腹部和臉部（眼睛周圍有條紋）是黃色的，而背部（有些黃色斑點）一直到頭頂和後部是黑色的。 它的腿和喙也是黑色的。 當接近一個月大時，鴨雛的羽毛開始變得單調，看起來更像雌性，但條紋更多，腿部失去深灰色的顏色。 孵化後兩個月，雛鴨即長成為亞成鳥。 雛鴨在孵化後50-60天即可飛翔。它的喙很快失去深灰色的顏色，性別可以通過三個地方來區分：1） 雄性的喙是黃色的，但雌性的是黑色和橙色的； 2） 雄性的胸羽是紅棕色的，但雌性的是棕色的； 3） 雄性的中央尾羽（雄鴨羽毛）是卷曲的，但雌性的是直的。 在成鳥之前的最後一段時期（6-10 個月大），雌性幼鴨的羽毛保持不變，而雄性幼鴨的羽毛逐漸變成其特有的顏色。 這種羽毛變化也可見於雄性綠頭鴨成鳥，當它們在夏季換羽期開始和結束時，長出和脫掉其非繁殖期的過渡羽毛。 綠頭鴨的成年年齡為十四個月，平均壽命為三年，但它們可以活到二十年。
有幾種雌鴨的羽毛是棕色的，可能與雌性綠頭鴨混淆。 雌性赤頸鴨（Mareca strepera）有一條橙色邊的喙，白色的腹部，黑白相間的翼鏡，在飛行中看起來像翅膀上的一個白色方塊，並且是一種較小的鳥。 在北美洲，與雌性綠頭鴨更相似的是綠嘴黑鴨（A. rubripes），其兩性的羽色比綠頭鴨明顯更深， 以及斑點鴨（A. fulvigula），其顏色比雌性綠頭鴨稍暗，裸露部位的顏色略有不同，且翼鏡上沒有白邊。

 在圈養中，家鴨的羽毛有野生型、白色和其他顏色。 這些顏色變異在不作為家畜飼養的綠頭鴨中也有發現，如作為寵物鳥、鳥園飼養等，這些顏色變異稀少但逐漸增多。
這是一種吵鬧的鳥，雌性有深沉的quack，通常與鴨子聯繫在一起。 雌性經常發出連續2-10聲的quacks，從大聲開始，音量逐漸減小。 雄性綠頭鴨發出的聲音在語音上類似於雌鴨的典型quack，但比雌鴨的聲音更深沉和安靜。米德爾塞克斯大學對英國兩個綠頭鴨種群的研究發現，綠頭鴨的聲音根據環境不同而變化，並具有類似區域口音的特徵，生活在倫敦的城市綠頭鴨比生活在康沃爾的鄉村綠頭鴨更響亮、更愛吵鬧，這是對持續的人工噪音水平的適應。
當在孵卵或有後代時，雌鴨會發出不同的聲音，聽起來像是通常鴨叫聲的縮短版。這種母性叫聲對其幼鴨具有很強的吸引力。這些quacks的重複和頻率調製構成了幼鴨同種識別的聽覺基礎，這個過程被稱為聲音的同種識別。 此外，當巢或幼鴨受到威脅或干擾時，雌鴨會發出嘶嘶聲。當起飛時，綠頭鴨的翅膀會發出特有的微弱的呼嘯聲。
綠頭鴨是一個少見的既符合艾倫法則又符合伯格曼法則的鳥類例子。 伯格曼法則指出，極地形態通常比來自較暖氣候的相關形態更大，這在鳥類中有許多例子，如格陵蘭綠頭鴨比南方的綠頭鴨更大。 艾倫法則說，像耳朵這樣的附肢在極地形態中通常較小以最小化熱量損失，而在熱帶和沙漠形態中則較大以促進熱量散失，極地亞種整體上更為粗壯。 這一法則在鳥類中的例子很少，因為鳥類缺乏外耳，但鴨子的喙有少量血管以防止熱量流失， 而且像格陵蘭綠頭鴨一樣，喙比南方鳥類的小，體現了這一法則。
由於綠頭鴨基因代碼的多樣性，這使它具有廣泛的雜交能力，決定羽色的基因突變非常常見，並產生了各種各樣的雜交種，如布魯爾鴨（綠頭鴨×赤頸鴨，Mareca strepera）。


分佈和棲地
綠頭鴨廣泛分布於北半球和南半球；在北美洲，其範圍從阿拉斯加南部和中部延伸到墨西哥、夏威夷群島， 橫跨古北界， 西邊是冰島 和格陵蘭南部 和摩洛哥（北非）的部分地區 ，北邊是斯堪的納維亞 和英國 ，以及西伯利亞， 日本， 和南韓。 另外在東部，範圍延伸到南半球的東南部和西南部澳大利亞 以及紐西蘭。  它在其繁殖範圍的北部地區具有強烈的遷徙性，並在更南的地方過冬。 例如，在北美洲，它在美國南部和墨西哥北部過冬， 但也經常在9月至5月間偶爾進入中美洲和加勒比地區。 一隻後來被命名為“特雷弗”的雄鴨在2018年引起媒體關注，因為它出現在紐埃島，這是綠頭鴨的不尋常地點。
綠頭鴨棲息於多種棲地和氣候區，從北極苔原到亞熱帶地區。 它出現在淡水和鹹水濕地，包括公園、小池塘、河流、湖泊和河口，以及海岸線附近的淺灣和開放海域。 偏好水深小於0.9米（3.0英尺）的區域，避開深度超過幾米的區域。 它們被帶有水生植物的水體吸引。


行為


覓食
綠頭鴨是雜食性動物，對食物選擇非常靈活。 其飲食可能根據多種因素而變化，包括繁殖週期的階段、可用食物的短期變化、營養素供應以及種間競爭和種內競爭。 綠頭鴨的飲食大部分似乎由腹足綱動物， 昆蟲（包括甲蟲、蒼蠅、鱗翅目昆蟲、蜻蜓和石蠅）， 甲殼類動物， 其他節肢動物， 蟲子， 其他鳥類的糞便， 許多種類的種子和植物材料， 以及根和塊莖組成。 在繁殖季節，記錄到雄鴨食用了37.6%的動物性食物和62.4%的植物性食物，特別是稗草（Echinochloa crus-galli）， 而未產卵的雌鴨食用了37.0%的動物性食物和63.0%的植物性食物，而產卵的雌鴨則食用了71.9%的動物性食物和僅28.1%的植物性食物。 植物通常構成鳥飲食的較大部分，特別是在秋季遷徙和冬季。
綠頭鴨通常透過撈取植物食物或覓食來進食；有報導稱它們食用青蛙、其他兩棲動物和魚，包括腐肉。 然而，2017年在羅馬尼亞觀察到一群綠頭鴨獵捕小型遷徙鳥類，包括灰鶺鴒和黑喉石䳭，這是它們首次被記錄到攻擊和食用大型脊椎動物。 它通常在河岸上築巢，但不一定在水邊。在繁殖季節之外是偏好群居鳥類，形成大型鳥群，稱為“sordes”。


繁殖

綠頭鴨通常在北半球的十月和十一月形成配對，直到雌性在築巢季節開始產卵之前。 此時，雄鴨會離開雌鴨，與其他雄鴨聚集等待六月（在北半球）開始的換羽期。 然而，在這短暫的時間內，雄鴨仍然具有性能力，其中一些會等待那些失去或放棄前一窩的雌鴨； 或強行與看似孤立或未配對的雌鴨交配，不論其種類以及是否有雛鴨。

築巢地點通常在地面，隱藏在植被中，雌鴨的斑點羽毛提供了有效的偽裝， 但也有雌鴨在樹洞、船屋、屋頂花園和陽台上築巢，有時導致孵化的雛鳥難以跟隨母親到水邊。 一窩蛋數量為8-13枚，蛋的顏色從乳白色到綠色斑駁。 它們的大小約為58 mm（2.3英寸）長和32 mm（1.3英寸）寬。 蛋是隔天產下的，孵化在整窩蛋幾乎產完時開始。 孵化期為27-28天，長出成鳥羽毛需50-60天。 雛鳥為早熟型，孵化後即可完全游泳。 然而，銘印使它們本能地靠近母親，不僅是為了溫暖和保護，還是為了學習並記住它們的棲地以及如何和在哪裡覓食。 雖然已知會有收養情況，但雌鴨通常不容忍陌生的雛鳥靠近牠們的雛鳥群，並會猛烈攻擊並驅逐任何不熟悉的幼鳥，有時甚至會殺死它們。
當雛鳥成長到能夠飛行的幼鳥時，它們會學習並記住其傳統的遷徙路線（除非它們在圈養中出生和長大）。在紐西蘭，綠頭鴨被歸化，發現其繁殖季節較長，蛋較大和數量較多，且幼雛存活率普遍高於其原產地的綠頭鴨。
如果育雛失敗，一些綠頭鴨可能會在初夏至仲夏期間第二次交配，試圖撫養第二窩蛋。此外，在不合季節的溫暖天氣情況下，綠頭鴨有時會在秋季繁殖；2011年11月，在倫敦濕地中心，一隻雌鴨成功孵化並撫養了一窩11隻雛鳥。
在繁殖季節，雄鴨和雌鴨都會變得具有攻擊性，通過衝向對方來驅逐競爭對手。 雄鴨比雌鴨更常打鬥，並通過不斷啄對手的胸部來攻擊對方，有時甚至拔掉羽毛和皮膚。雌鴨也會進行“煽動展示（inciting displays）”，鼓勵鴨群中的其他鴨子開始打鬥。 這種行為可能允許雌鴨評估潛在伴侶的強度。
那些在其他鴨子已經配對後被遺棄的雄鴨，有時會鎖定一隻孤立的雌鴨，即使是不同種的雌鴨，並追逐和啄咬她直到她虛弱，這時雄鴨會輪流與雌鴨交配。 Lebret（1961年）稱這種行為為“企圖強姦飛行”，而史丹利·克蘭普和K.E.L. Simmons（1977年）稱之為“強姦意圖飛行”。 雄鴨也偶爾以相同方式追逐其他不同種的雄鴨，甚至是彼此。 在一次記錄的“同性戀戀屍”事件中，一隻雄鴨在被追逐的雄鴨撞上玻璃窗後死亡，隨後與死去的雄鴨交配。 這篇論文在2003年獲得了搞笑諾貝爾獎。
綠頭鴨經常成為巢寄生的目標，偶爾有美洲潛鴨、赤麻鴨、小斑背潛鴨、赤膀鴨、琵嘴鴨、尖尾鴨、桂紅鴨、普通秋沙鴨和其他綠頭鴨在其巢中產卵。 如果寄生發生在產卵期間，雌鴨可能會試圖將它們排出或甚至放棄巢。


捕食者和威脅

除了人類的狩獵，綠頭鴨在所有年齡段（尤其是年幼的）和所有地點都必須面對各種掠食者，包括猛禽和貓頭鷹、鼬科動物、鴉科動物、蛇、浣熊、負鼠、臭鼬、烏龜、大型魚類、貓科動物和犬科動物，最後兩者包括家貓和家犬。 成鴨最主要的自然掠食者是赤狐（Vulpes vulpes；最常獵捕正在孵化的雌鴨） 和較快或較大的猛禽 （例如遊隼、金鵰 或白頭海鵰）。 在北美，成鴨面臨不下15種猛禽的威脅，從北方澤鵟（Circus hudsonius）和短耳鴞（Asio flammeus）（兩者都比綠頭鴨小）到巨大的白頭海鵰（Haliaeetus leucocephalus）和金鵰（Aquila chrysaetos），以及大約12種哺乳類的掠食者，這還不包括幾種威脅蛋和雛鳥的鳥類和哺乳類掠食者。
綠頭鴨還被其他水邊的頂級掠食者捕食，如蒼鷺（Ardea cinerea）、 大藍鷺（Ardea herodias） 和夜鷺（Nycticorax nycticorax）、黑脊鷗（Larus argentatus）、鯰魚（Silurus glanis） 和白斑狗魚（Esox lucius）。 烏鴉（Corvus）  也偶爾會殺死雛鴨和成鴨。 此外，綠頭鴨可能在繁殖季節被較大的雁形目鳥類如天鵝（Cygnus ） 和鵝攻擊，並經常因為領土爭端被這些鳥類驅趕。疣鼻天鵝（Cygnus olor） 已知會攻擊甚至殺死綠頭鴨，如果它們覺得鴨子對其後代構成威脅。 普通潛鳥（Gavia inmer） 也是類似地對其他鳥類在這些爭端中具有領域性和攻擊性，並經常將綠頭鴨驅趕出它們的領地。然而，在2019年，威斯康辛州的一對普通潛鳥被觀察到撫養了一隻綠頭鴨雛鳥數周，似乎在其父母被遺棄後收養了這隻鳥。
在夏季，高溫和水位下降的組合使綠頭鴨更容易感染肉毒桿菌中毒，因為這些條件對肉毒桿菌的繁殖是理想的，這些鳥類也更有可能接觸到細菌產生的肉毒桿菌毒素。綠頭鴨群體中的肉毒桿菌中毒爆發可能導致大規模死亡。
綠頭鴨的捕食規避行為，如睜一隻眼睛睡覺，使大腦一半保持警覺而另一半睡覺，首次在綠頭鴨中得到了證明，儘管一般認為這在鳥類中是普遍的。


狀態和保護

自1998年以來，綠頭鴨在IUCN瀕危物種紅色名錄中被評為無危物種。這是因為它的分佈範圍廣——超過20,000,000平方公里（7,700,000平方英里）  並且其群體數量在增加，而不是在十年或三代內減少30%，因此不需要易危評級。此外，綠頭鴨的群體數量非常大。
與許多水禽不同，綠頭鴨從人類對世界的改變中受益匪淺，以至於它們現在在某些地區被視為入侵物種。 在它們棲息的地區，它們經常出現在城市公園、湖泊、池塘和其他人造水域中，並且由於它們對人類的溫順性質和美麗的虹彩顏色，通常在人類棲地中被容忍或鼓勵。 雖然大多數未被馴化，但綠頭鴨如此成功地與人類共存，以至於它們在保護方面的主要風險來自一旦人類和綠頭鴨在某一地區定居，當地傳統鴨類的遺傳多樣性會減少。綠頭鴨非常適應環境，能夠在城市地區生活甚至繁榮，這些地區在發展之前可能支持更多本地化、敏感的水禽物種。 在它們非本地的地方放生野生綠頭鴨有時會通過與當地的水禽雜交而產生問題。 這些非遷徙的綠頭鴨與當地族群中的近親物種的本地野鴨雜交，通過基因污染產生可繁殖後代。 各種野鴨基因庫的完全雜交可能導致許多本地水禽的滅絕。 綠頭鴨本身是大多數家鴨的祖先，其自然演化的野生基因庫反過來被畜養和野生群體所污染。
隨著時間的推移，發展出介於兩個物種之間的雜交種群；物種形成過程開始逆轉。 這引起了對綠頭鴨親屬的保護生物學擔憂，如夏威夷鴨、 紐西蘭灰鴨（A. s. superciliosa） 太平洋黑鴨的亞種）、 綠嘴黑鴨、 斑點鴨、 麻斑鴨、 黃嘴鴨、 和墨西哥鴨， 在後者的情況下甚至引發了關於這些鳥應該被視為一個物種（因此有資格獲得更多的保護研究和資金）還是包括在綠頭鴨物種中的爭議。生態變化和狩獵也導致了本地物種的下降；例如，紐西蘭灰鴨的群體在20世紀中葉因過度捕獵而急劇下降。 夏威夷鴨的雜交後代似乎不太適應本地棲地，使用它們進行重新引入項目似乎降低了成功率。 總而言之，綠頭鴨“雜交掉”近似種的問題，不如說是當地鴨子數量減少的結果，而不是綠頭鴨的擴散；異域物種形成和隔離行為產生了今天的綠頭鴨類鴨類的多樣性，儘管在大多數（如果不是全部）的這些群體中，雜交一定在某種程度上發生過。


侵入性
 綠頭鴨與本地鴨類繁殖，對南非的生物多樣性造成嚴重的“基因污染”，儘管保護當地水禽種群的協議 – 同樣適用於綠頭鴨和其他鴨類。 綠頭鴨與黃嘴鴨的雜交種是可生育的，能夠生產混合後代。 如果這種情況持續下去，將只會出現雜交種，從長遠來看會導致各種本地水禽的滅絕。 綠頭鴨可以與63種其他物種雜交，對本地水禽的遺傳完整性構成嚴重威脅。 綠頭鴨及其雜交種與本地鳥類競爭資源，包括巢位、棲地和食物。
在美國，綠頭鴨、綠頭鴨雛鳥和受精的綠頭鴨蛋可供公眾出售和私人擁有，無論是作為家禽還是寵物，目前都是合法的，除了佛羅里達州，該州目前禁止家養綠頭鴨。這是為了防止與本地的斑點鴨雜交。
在澳大利亞和紐西蘭，綠頭鴨被視為入侵物種，它們與太平洋黑鴨（在紐西蘭當地稱為灰鴨）競爭，後者在過去曾被過度捕獵。在那裡以及其他地方，隨著城市化的增加，綠頭鴨在擴散並與當地的親屬雜交。
花嘴鴨目前正在侵入濱海邊疆區的綠頭鴨族群中，可能是由於全球變暖引起的生境變化。 馬里亞納綠頭鴨是一個獨立的異域物種族群，在大多數方面是一個優良的物種，顯然最初是由綠頭鴨-太平洋黑鴨的雜交產生的； 它在20世紀末滅絕。
來珊鴨是綠頭鴨的一個島嶼親屬，數量非常少且波動很大。 綠頭鴨有時會在遷徙期間到達其島嶼家園，並且可以預期在這些物種存在的時間內偶爾會留下來並與來珊鴨雜交。 然而，這些雜交種對萊桑島獨特的生態條件適應性較差，因此適應度較低。在公元400 年之前，來珊鴨遍布夏威夷群島，隨後在波利尼西亞殖民期間迅速衰落。 現在，它們的分佈範圍僅限於萊桑島。 它是成功遷徙的鳥類之一，在20世紀初幾乎滅絕後得以恢復。


與人類的關係


馴化

綠頭鴨經常出現在人類的公園、農場和其他人工水道的池塘、河流和溪流中 – 甚至會造訪人類的庭院水景。
綠頭鴨與人類有著悠久的關係。幾乎所有的家鴨品種都源自綠頭鴨，除了少數疣鼻棲鴨品種， 並且都列在三名法之下。綠頭鴨通常是單配偶制，而家鴨大多是多配偶制。家鴨沒有領土行為，比綠頭鴨更不具攻擊性。 家鴨主要是為了肉而飼養的；它們的蛋也可以食用，並且味道濃郁。 它們最早在至少4,000年前的新石器時代的東南亞被馴化，並且也被羅馬人在歐洲和馬來人在亞洲飼養。 由於家鴨和綠頭鴨是同一物種，因此綠頭鴨與家鴨交配並產生完全可育的雜交後代是很常見的。 因此，綠頭鴨的基因已被家鴨的基因污染。
儘管飼養家禽品種更受歡迎，但有時也會飼養純種綠頭鴨以獲取蛋和肉， 雖然它們可能需要剪羽以限制飛行。


狩獵

由於數量龐大，綠頭鴨是最常被獵杀的鴨子品種之一。理想的狩獵綠頭鴨地點被認為是水位較淺的地方，鳥類可以在那裡覓食。 在某些地方、某些時間和某些群體中，狩獵綠頭鴨可能會導致其數量下降。 在某些國家，綠頭鴨可以合法狩獵，但受到國家法律和政策的保護。例如，在英國，綠頭鴨受1981年野生動植物和鄉村法案的保護，該法案限制了某些狩獵、捕捉和殺害綠頭鴨的方式。


作為食物
自古以來，綠頭鴨就被當作食物食用。在新石器時代的希臘，野生綠頭鴨被食用。 通常只吃胸肉和大腿肉。 它不需要在準備前風乾，通常是燉或烤，有時用苦橙或波特酒調味。


在文化中
《讓路給小鴨子》是由羅勃·麥羅斯基撰寫和插圖的兒童繪本。這本書以一對在波士頓公共花園養育小鴨子的綠頭鴨為中心。
《飛鴨向前衝》是一部由環球影業和照明娛樂製作的動畫冒險喜劇電影。故事講述了一個綠頭鴨家庭試圖從新英格蘭遷徙到牙買加的過程。
